coredata.dc:title: All-in-one, reversible yellow-green color switching tungsten oxide electrochromic devices
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Zhichao
Li, Qiang
Yu, Mingfu
Lv, Ying
Sun, Hong
Zhang, Yongqiang
Wang, Dandan
coredata.dc:description: Tungsten oxide (WO3) sol doped with carbon nanodots (CDs) was utilized for constructing an electrolyte-free, all-in-one electrochromic device (ECD). Different from the traditional WO3-based ECDs, showing the color transition between colorless and blue, the demonstrated device could realize reversible color changes between yellow and green due to the intrinsic color of CDs. The optical contrast of the optimized ECD was up to 53.6% at 650 nm and the coloration response time was 27 s. It could also be automatically bleached under 0 V with the bleaching response time of 80 s. In addition, attributing to the unique photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of CDs, the ECD could also present tunable PL under various excitation wavelengths, which potentially broadens the applications of ECDs in multiple anti-counterfeiting. These results provide simple and effective guidelines for constructing high-performance ECDs with simple architecture and extended color palette.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrolyte-free
WO3
Electrochromic
Carbon nanodot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Transmittance and fluorescence properties of the device

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmittance spectra of ECD-1∼4 with different concentrations of stabilizer in bleached state.
PL spectra of CDs doped WO3 sol (a) with and (b) without ethanol stabilizer at various excitation wavelengths.
CV curve of ECD-3 at the scan rate of 50 mV⋅s−1.
(a) Transmittance spectra of ECD-3 in colored and bleached states; (b) Current response curve and corresponding transmittance curve of ECD-3 at potential of −2 V and 0 V.

Optical transmittance and chromaticity of ECD-3 in the colored and bleached states; insets are the photographs of ECD-3 in colored state, bleached state, and under the excitation of 254-nm UV light. (For interpretation of the colors in the figure(s), the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Illustration of an all-in-one electrochromic device based on a carbon-dots doped WO3 sol.

head.ce:title: All-in-one, reversible yellow-green color switching tungsten oxide electrochromic devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of science, Shenyang Jianzhu University, Shenyang, 110168, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of science, Shenyang Jianzhu University, Shenyang, 110168, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Shenyang Jianzhu University, Shenyang, 110168, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: chool of Mechanical Engineering, Shenyang Jianzhu University, Shenyang, 110168, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications, Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications
Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications, Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130033, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Optoelectronic Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Optoelectronic Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tungsten oxide (WO3) sol doped with carbon nanodots (CDs) was utilized for constructing an electrolyte-free, all-in-one electrochromic device (ECD). Different from the traditional WO3-based ECDs, showing the color transition between colorless and blue, the demonstrated device could realize reversible color changes between yellow and green due to the intrinsic color of CDs. The optical contrast of the optimized ECD was up to 53.6% at 650 nm and the coloration response time was 27 s. It could also be automatically bleached under 0 V with the bleaching response time of 80 s. In addition, attributing to the unique photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of CDs, the ECD could also present tunable PL under various excitation wavelengths, which potentially broadens the applications of ECDs in multiple anti-counterfeiting. These results provide simple and effective guidelines for constructing high-performance ECDs with simple architecture and extended color palette.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochromic (EC) materials and devices (ECDs) have drawn growing attention due to their stable and reversible optical modulation properties [1–4] as well as their verified utility in achieving energy-saving displays, smart windows/mirrors [5,6], military camouflage and other smart systems [7,8]. The architecture of most ECDs is 5-layer-type configuration containing stacked transparent conductive film, electrochromic layer, electrolyte, ion storage layer and opposite transparent conductive film. Apart from the EC materials [9–12], developing competitive electrolytes is crucial to realizing high-performance ECDs [13]. During the electrochemical redox process, electrolyte guarantee rapid ion transfer between the two electrodes and to balance the charged species generated during the redox processes. Generally, electrolytes comprise metal salts and organic polar solvents with high dielectric constant and low viscosity to facilitate the ion migration. However, metal ions in the electrolyte have a risk of being reduced into metal upon numerous cycling. The continuous growth of metal between the two electrode would easily cause short circuit, reducing the low-term stability of ECDs and restrict their industrialization. In addition, most high-performance metal-salts-based electrolytes are flammable, explosive and dangerous, so it is still full of challenges in developing nontoxic electrolytes with perfect homogeneity, high ionic conductivity, zero electronic conductivity as well as good electrochemical and thermal stability.

Herein, WO3 sol doped with CDs (CDs-WO3 sol) were utilized to contrast an electrolyte-free, low-cost, all-in-one ECD. This design not only simplifies the device architecture, reducing the fabrication cost, but also improves the stability and safety of ECDs. Most importantly, a reversible color change between yellow and green was achieved by utilizing the optimized CDs-WO3 sol, which significantly enriches the color modulation range of WO3 considering traditional WO3-based ECDs ordinarily show adaptable color between colorless and blue. In addition, the luminescence property of CDs also endows the device with multi-wavelength fluorescence emission performance, which lays a foundation for the application of the device in display, military camouflage, information encryption fields.

Ion exchange resin, hydrated sodium tungstate (Na2WO
⋅
4
2 H2O, ≥99%), anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, ≥99.5%), citric acid (C6H8O
⋅
7
H2O, ≥99.5%)), urea (CH4N2O, ≥99.5%) were purchased from Merck and used without further purification. Tin-doped indium oxide glass electrode (ITO, surface resistance 20 Ω/□) were purchased from CSG Holding CO., LTD. Deionized water with the resistivity of 18.2 MΩ⋅cm was obtained from Millipore ultra-pure water purification system.

WO3 sol was prepared by ion exchange method according to previously reported procedure with minor modification [14,15]. The commercial ion exchange resin was washed with distilled water and packed uniformly in a glass column. The hydrated sodium tungstate (Na2WO
⋅
4
2 H2O) was dissolved in deionized water (the resistivity is 18.2 MΩ⋅cm) to form a transparent solution with the concentration of 0.5 M. The above solution was allowed to flow down through the glass column at the rate about one drop per second, and the effluent was WO3 sol, which is transparent and pale-yellow colored sol. The typical Tyndall effect can be observed in the WO3 sol.

The required CDs in this study have been reported by our group [16,17] and synthesized by microwave method. In a specific preparation method [18] 3 g urea and 3 g citric acid were added to 10 mL distilled water to form a colorless and transparent solution and then the solution was heated in a microwave oven (750 W) for about 5 min. At last, dark-brown clustered solid, indicating the formation of CDs. The synthesized CDs were transferred to a vacuum oven, and heated at 60°C for 1 h, and then stored in a vacuum drying oven at room temperature.

Organic reagents or dispersants are often dissolved into WO3 sol to improve its stability [19,20]. In this test, ethanol as the stabilizer was added into WO3 sol, and the volume ratios WO3 sol and ethanol was 5:1, 5:3, 1:1 and 2:3, respectively. Then solid CDs with mass ratio (m-WO3 sol: m-CDs) of 200:3 was added to the above solution under intense stirring. After being dispersed and centrifugal separated from trace undissolved material at high speed of 6000 r/min, the prepared CDs-WO3 sols were stored at room temperature for further use. We noted that the stability of WO3 sol was significantly improved after adding CDs and ethanol, and sediment didn't appear after being placed at room temperature for 3 months.

ECDs preparation was as follows: The 18 cm×18 cm glass substrates with ITO electrode (surface resistance 20 Ω/□) were ultrasonically cleaned with mixture of ether and ethanol (V/V=1/1) for 15 min, and finally dried under an oven lamp (250 W). Then two ITO electrodes are bonded together with a heat-sealing film. The prepared CD-WO3 sol was injected into an encapsulated box containing two ITO electrodes through an injection hole. They were named as ECD-1∼4, respectively. All ECDs in this work were encapsulated with a heat-sealing film with the thickness of 120 μm. They have the same thickness, which can exclude the influence of the thickness difference of each device on the evaluation of their EC performances.

The transmittance spectra of the samples were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrophotometer. The electrochemical and EC behavior of the devices were studied in a CHI 920 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Inc., China) equipped with an Ocean Optics QE–Pro spectrometer using a standard three–electrode arrangement in a quartz electrochemical cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmittance and fluorescence properties of the device
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, excellent initial transmittance for ECDs is highly desired to achieving high optical modulation. Fig. 1 shows the transmittance spectra of ECD-1∼4. It can be seen that the ECD-1∼4 exhibited strong absorption over the ultraviolet band (300–420 nm) due to the existence of CDs [16,17]. However, in the visible light range (400–800 nm), the average transmittances of ECD-1∼4 were all >70%, which meets the general requirements for the transparency of smart windows. As the increase of ethanol, the average transmittance of ECD increases slightly. This is because the ethanol servers as a solvent to dilute the solution and reduce the absorption.

Contributed by the excellent luminescent performance of CDs [16,17], the prepared ECDs also show tunable emission properties. Fig. 2 shows the PL spectra of CD-WO3 sol at various excitation wavelengths. Clearly, the fluorescence spectra of the CDs-WO3 sol depends on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2a), which is consistent well with the PL spectra of CDs solution [16,17]. A large red-shift in the PL peak together with the great variation in PL intensity was realized with the increase of excitation wavelength from 340 to 460 nm. The strongest fluorescence emission peak at 510 nm was observed at 420 nm excitation, while under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the second strongest fluorescence emission peak at 490 nm was obtained. In contrast, without CDs, WO3 sol shows no fluorescence (Fig. S1), suggesting that the unique PL emission of CDs-WO3 sol derived from CDs.

The CDs doped WO3 sol tends to precipitate within a few hours due to its poor stability. However, after adding ethanol, the stability was significantly improved. Sediment didn't appear even after being stored at room temperature for 3 months. Fig. 2b displays PL spectra of CDs doped WO3 sol after adding ethanol. Obviously, ethanol doesn't affect the PL peak position of CDs doped WO3 sol and but slight changes the PL emission intensity. The strongest fluorescence emission peak was obtained at 448 nm at 340 nm excitation in CD-WO3 sol. The ECDs with tunable PL emission can also be applied in the field of display, military camouflage, information encryption.

In order to find the proper driving voltages to evaluate the overall electrochromic performances of the fabricated all-in-one ECDs, we directly measured the CV curves by utilizing the ECDs as the electrochemical cells and the two identical ITO electrodes in the ECD as the working and counter electrode, respectively. That is various from the traditional three electrode electrochemical system containing another stable reference electrode. Fig. 3 illustrates the cyclic voltammetry (CV) trends of ECD-3 at the scan rate of 50 mV⋅s−1 and the CV curves for other ECDs are list in Fig. S2 in supporting information. We can see that all the CV curves were highly symmetric, which could be attributed to the symmetrically anodic and cathodic potential. The cathode peak appears at about −0.45 V (vs. ITO) can be related to the reduction (colored state) of WO3 sol. The optical contrast was calculated by the difference between the transmittance of the device in a fully-colored state and that in a fully-bleached state. The transmittance of ECD-1∼4 at potential of −2 V and 0 V were further measured and shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S3. It can be seen that the maximum optical contrast of the devices were all above 44.1% and could be adjusted by changing the proportion of components in the solution (Fig. S3). The maximum optical contrast of 53.6% was achieved on ECD-3.

One of the most important characteristics of ECDs was the response time needed to perform a switching between colored and bleached states. The coloration and bleaching response time were defined as the time required for 90% conversion of total optical contrast. The coloration response time of ECD-3 was 27 s, calculated from Fig. 4b. When the applied voltage was 0 V, the device can gradually recover from the colored state to the bleached state with a response time about 80 s. It needs approximately 90 s to the initial state. The performance curves of other ECDs are shown in Fig. S4 and Table S1. It can be seen the electrochemical comprehensive performance of ECD-3 was better than other devices. We also studied the electrochemical cycling stabilities of the ECD-3. Fig. S4 displays the curves of transmittance at 650 nm versus time for the ECD-3 under the first five and the last five electrochemical cycling. We can see that the average transmittance modulation between bleached state and colored state in the first five cycles was 57.7%, which still remains at approximately 54.9% after 100 successive cycles without any significant decline, showing a good electrochemical cycling stability.

According to the transmittance curve of the device in different states, the chromaticity and luminous transmittance of the devices are calculated [21]. It can be seen from Fig. 5 and Fig. S5 that the chromaticity of this group of devices in the bleached state was basically similar. About 82.8% luminous transmittance and (0.43, 0.47) color coordinate were achieved on this bright yellow device. However, the coloration luminous transmittance and chromaticity basically lied in the green region, differed greatly under the same voltage. As the applied potential increased, the tint of the device deepens and even reached emerald green. A 29.2% luminous transmittance and (0.32, 0.45) color coordinate achieved on bright green ECD-3 at the applied potential of −2 V. These results were consistent with the color change under natural light in the photo (the insert in Fig. 5).

Generally, most WO3-based ECDs can only show reversible color changes between colorless and blue, whereas the color of our CDs doped WO3-based ECDs in bleached state is yellow [21–24]. When a small driving potential of −2.0 V was given to the device, the device turned green. That was in accordance with the principle of “subtractive primaries” [25], in which two different colors will mix with each other to form a third color. Therefore, the overlay of yellow CDs and blue WO3 with colored state produces green.

The above analysis suggested that WO3-based ECD successfully realized reversible color change between yellow and green by adding CDs. It not only enriches the color change properties, but also simplifies the structure of the device. The yellow-green reversible change characteristic of device makes it have a great application prospect in military target camouflage. In addition, the WO3-based ECD is endowed with fluorescent properties, due to the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs. The insert in Fig. 5 shows a glowing ECD-3 under a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp. The device, which has both fluorescent and electrochromic functions, is also expected to realize encrypt multiply information, and to be used in anti-counterfeiting field.

For comparison, WO3 sol without CDs was also used to fabricate the device to study the electrochromic nature of WO3-based ECD with non-metallic salt electrolyte. However, no color changes were observed under the driving voltage of −2 V∼0 V, which indicated that no electrochemical reaction and no electrochromic phenomenon in WO3-based devices without CDs and electrolyte. The comparison of the CV curve of ECD-3 in Fig. 3 indicated that CDs played a similar role as electrolyte in electrochromic reaction. During the electrochemical redox process, CDs may acted as movable ions due to their small size (average diameter was 3.7 nm, Fig. S6). Therefore, it was speculated that the electrochromic principle of this fluorescent WO3-based ECD was as follows (Scheme 1 ): When WO3 was reduced by capturing electrons into W5+, there was electrostatic attraction between W5+ and CDs rich in amino groups to keep local electrically neutral. As the applied voltage was 0 V, the electrostatic interaction between them disappeared, the device gradually restored to original color by the electrons escaping from WO3.

The electrolyte-free WO3-based ECD with all-in-one architecture was achieved by utilizing WO3 sol doped with CDs, which acted as movable ions to balance the charged W5+ during electrochemical redox process. In this paper, WO3-based ECD was endowed with richer color change properties by adding the yellow CDs, and finally realizing reversible yellow-green color switching. The optical contrast of the optimized device was up to 53.6% and the coloration response time was 27 s. It can automatically bleach under 0 V with the bleaching response time of 80 s. In addition, the device had PL properties and could emit different light colors at various excitation wavelengths, which shows application potential in many fields such as military camouflage and multi-encryption anti-counterfeiting.

Zhichao Xue & Ying Lv: Designed and performed the experiments. Writing - Original Draft. Qiang Li & Mingfu Yu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hong Sun: Directed, Supervised, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yongqiang Zhang: Validation, Mechanism analysis. Dandan Wang: Writing-Review & Editing. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Supported by Natural science fund guidance plan of Liaoning Province (2019-ZD-0686); Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1802045); National Natural Science Foundation of China (51776131, 51902211, 61875195); Open Project of the State Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Applications, SKLA-Z-2020-01.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physleta.2020.126822.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC
PL spectra; CV curves; transmittance spectra; in-situ transmittance, current response curve and corresponding transmittance curve; summary table of EC performance; chromaticity; and TEM images.
MMC
